title: Rigger Competency Slide Course
description: Classroom slide course for rigger competency — regulations, WLL/design factor, inspection, rigging math, below-the-hook, and lift planning. Aligned with BC Crane Safety and WorkSafeBC OHSR Part 15.

slides.title: Rigger competency
slides.summary: Rules, rigging stats, sharp edges, sling angles, sine math, L ÷ H, and why softeners.
bullets: Sharp edges · compressive forces · leg angles
Sine, L ÷ H, and two calculator quizzes

slides.title: The Rules
slides.summary: Law first — then adopted standards and manufacturer limits.

items.label: WorkSafeBC OHSR Part 14 — cranes & material handling

Employer procedure & manufacturer WLL govern

slides.title: Sharp edges — protect the sling
slides.summary: OHSR Part 15 — sharp corners cut synthetic and wire rope; pad the sling contact point.

items.label: Protect slings from sharp edges and corners on the load
Not the edge — protect the sling where it bears on the corner
Pads, sleeves, wood, or engineered protection at the contact point
Re-check softeners after the first few inches of lift — they can shift
Cut or crushed sling fibres — remove from service immediately

slides.focusCallout: Protect the sling — not the edge

slides.title: Compressive forces — sling angles
slides.summary: Shallow bridle — squeeze on the load and overload in each leg.

sections.heading: Compression
items: Each leg pushes inward on the load

items.label: Low angle = high squeeze

sections.heading: Sling tension
items: Shallow angle multiplies tension in each leg
Steeper bridle reduces both problems

slides.focusCallout: Low angle → squeeze + high leg tension

slides.title: Angles — 60°, 45°, and 30°
slides.summary: Steep legs hold more. Flat legs hold less.

items.label: 60° — ~87% capacity
45° — ~71% capacity

sections.heading: Flat legs

items.label: 30° — 50% capacity · double pull

items: Harder pull → less capacity

slides.focusCallout: Harder pull → less capacity

slides.title: Sling tension — sine
slides.summary: Steeper = easier. Flatter = harder. Harder = hold less.

sections.heading: The idea
items: Flat ropes = harder pull on each leg
Harder pull = less safe load

sections.heading: 45° example

items.label: ≈ 7 of 10 pounds safe

items: Steep is strong · flat is weak

slides.focusCallout: Flat angle = pull harder = lift less

slides.title: Calculator check — sine
slides.summary: Four quick problems in DEG mode. Enter each on your calculator before answers are revealed.

quizQuestions.prompt: Using a scientific calculator in Degrees (DEG), enter Sin(60). What number do you get?
quizQuestions.explanation: sin 60° ≈ 0.866 — used when checking sling leg tension at a 60° leg angle.

quizQuestions.prompt: Still in DEG mode, enter Sin(45). What number do you get?
quizQuestions.explanation: sin 45° ≈ 0.707 — matches the ≈7/10 safe-load example from the previous slide.

quizQuestions.prompt: In DEG mode, enter Sin(30). What number do you get?
quizQuestions.explanation: sin 30° = 0.500 — a 30° leg angle doubles tension per leg (T = W).
1 ÷ sin 60° ≈ 1.155 — the tension multiplier per leg at a 60° angle.

slides.title: Length ÷ height — 45°
slides.summary: At a 45° leg angle, compare sling length (L) to vertical height (H).

sections.heading: 45° example
items: Vertical height H = 10 ft · sling leg length L ≈ 14.1 ft
Both legs at 45° from horizontal — symmetric bridle

sections.heading: Tension

items.label: Length ÷ height → L ÷ H
14.1 ÷ 10 = 1.41 tension factor per leg

items: Pull harder than half the load — multiply weight by ~1.41

sections.heading: Reduction

items.label: Height ÷ length → H ÷ L

items: Flatter angle — less of your WLL is available

slides.focusCallout: L ÷ H = tension · H ÷ L = reduction

slides.title: L ÷ H quiz — 60°, 45°, 30°
slides.summary: Length ÷ height gives tension. Height ÷ length gives reduction. Work each before revealing.

quizQuestions.prompt: H = 10 ft, L = 11.55 ft (60° leg angle). What is L ÷ H?
H = 10 ft, L = 14.1 ft (45° leg angle). What is L ÷ H?
H = 10 ft, L = 20 ft (30° leg angle). What is L ÷ H?
H = 10 ft, L = 14.1 ft (45° leg angle). What is H ÷ L?

slides.summary: Wrap up the intro — tie angles, sine, and L ÷ H to protecting your sling at sharp corners.

sections.heading: You learned
items: Rules, angles, sine, and L ÷ H
Steeper legs → lower tension → more capacity

sections.heading: Why softeners

items.label: Sharp edges cut synthetic and wire rope — pad the sling
OHSR 15.39 — protect slings at the contact point

items: Pads, sleeves, and engineered protection before the lift

slides.focusCallout: Protect the sling — not just the corner

slides.title: Rigging Removal Criteria

sections.heading: Removal criteria
items: Worn, damaged, or altered rigging — remove from service
Broken wires, cracked hooks, missing tags, bad fittings

sections.heading: Before the lift

items.label: OHSR 15.31 — inspect before every use
If in doubt — tag out and do not use

items: Unmarked or makeshift hardware never starts safe

slides.focusCallout: Nothing lasts forever — some things never even start.

slides.title: What All Rigging Must Have

sections.heading: Every fitting must show
items: Manufacturer name or identification mark
Product identifier — size, grade, or part number

items.label: Working load limit (WLL) — or enough info to look it up

sections.heading: OHSR & standards

items.label: OHSR 15.5 — manufacturer's ID, product ID, and WLL
ASME B30.26 — rigging hardware identification markings

items: B30.9 slings — capacity tags; catalogue on site when WLL is not stamped

items.label: Unmarked gear — qualified person rates it or remove from service

slides.focusCallout: If you can't read it, you can't rate it.

slides.title: Removal Criteria
slides.summary: Remove the hook from service immediately if any of the following are found:

sections.heading: Remove immediately
items: Cracks, fractures, or unauthorized weld repairs
Throat opening increased by more than 5% (max 6 mm / ¼ in. unless manufacturer specifies otherwise)
More than 10% wear of the original cross-section
Any visible bend or twist from the original plane of the hook
Excessive corrosion, pitting, or other damage affecting strength

items.label: Damaged, missing, or non-functioning safety latch (where required)
Missing or illegible manufacturer or WLL markings

items: Any condition identified by the manufacturer as requiring removal

slides.focusCallout: When in doubt, tag it out. Never attempt to straighten, heat, weld, or repair a hook unless specifically permitted by the manufacturer and applicable standards.

slides.title: Removal Criteria
slides.summary: Remove the chain sling from service immediately if any of the following are found:
slides.source: ASME B30.9 — Slings · ASME B30.26 — Rigging Hardware · manufacturer inspection criteria

sections.heading: Remove immediately
items: Cracked, broken, bent, twisted, or stretched links
Gouges, nicks, or excessive wear on any link
Wear exceeding 10% of the original link diameter
Elongation or deformation beyond the manufacturer's allowable limits
Evidence of heat damage — discoloration, weld spatter, arc strikes, or high-temperature exposure

items.label: Severe corrosion or pitting that reduces the chain's strength
Damaged or missing identification tag — grade, size, WLL, or serial number

items: Damaged hooks, master links, coupling links, or other sling components
Any condition identified by the manufacturer as requiring removal

slides.focusCallout: Never weld, heat, straighten, or repair alloy hoisting chain unless performed by the manufacturer or a qualified repair facility authorized by the manufacturer.

slides.title: Minimum Chain Grade
slides.summary: Minimum grade for overhead lifting
slides.source: ASME B30.9 — Slings and manufacturer requirements

sections.heading: Acceptable for hoisting
items: Grade 80 or higher
Grade 80 alloy chain
Grade 100 alloy chain
Grade 120 alloy chain
Any higher-grade alloy chain specifically rated and marked for overhead lifting

sections.heading: Not acceptable for overhead lifting
items: Grade 30 proof coil chain
Grade 43 high test chain
Grade 70 transport chain

items.label: Any chain not marked with the proper grade and WLL

slides.focusCallout: Grade 80 is the minimum alloy chain grade for overhead lifting. Grade 70 transport chain is for load securement, not hoisting.

slides.title: Required Identification Tag
slides.summary: Under ASME B30.9 and common manufacturer requirements, a chain bridle sling must have a permanently affixed, durable identification tag.

sections.heading: Tag must show
items: Manufacturer's name or trademark
Manufacturer's code or stock number
Chain grade (e.g., Grade 80, 100, or 120)
Chain size (nominal diameter)
Number of sling legs
Reach (length)

items.label: Rated Working Load Limit (WLL) for the applicable hitch(es) and sling angle(s)
Tag is missing or illegible

slides.focusCallout: This is one of the few sling types where reach (length) and number of legs are specifically required as part of the sling identification because they directly affect the sling's rated capacity.

slides.title: Understanding Bridle Sling Capacity
slides.source: WorkSafeBC OHS Regulation 15.33; ASME B30.9 — Slings

sections.heading: Capacity
items: Bridle slings may have 2, 3, or 4 legs

items.label: The load is not always shared equally between all legs

items: Sling angle has a significant effect on leg tension and overall capacity

items.label: For WLL calculations, a 4-leg bridle sling is rated as a 3-leg sling

items: The WLL of the sling assembly is limited by the lowest-rated component
Every leg, fitting, and master link must have adequate capacity for the lift

slides.focusCallout: Never assume all four legs carry the load equally. Always calculate the lift using the applicable sling angle and the rated capacity of a three-leg bridle.

slides.title: Basic Load Calculations
slides.summary: Always calculate load share, tension multiplier, and actual leg tension — then verify the leg WLL exceeds the calculated tension.

sections.heading: 1. Load Share
items: Load per Leg = Total Load ÷ Number of Loaded Legs
Example: 6,000 lb ÷ 2 = 3,000 lb per leg (before sling angle is considered)

sections.heading: 2. Tension Multiplier (Derating)
items: Leg Tension = Load per Leg × Tension Multiplier
Tension Multiplier = L ÷ H
or Tension Multiplier = 1 ÷ sin(θ)
L = Sling Length · H = Vertical Height · θ = Sling Angle (from horizontal)

sections.heading: Remember
items: Load Share
Tension Multiplier
Actual Leg Tension
Verify the leg WLL exceeds the calculated tension

slides.focusCallout: As the sling angle decreases, leg tension increases. A smaller angle = greater derating and higher forces in each sling leg.

slides.title: Common Sling Angles
slides.summary: Leg Tension = (Load ÷ Number of Loaded Legs) × Tension Multiplier
slides.source: WorkSafeBC OHSR Table 15-3 · ASME B30.9 — Slings

items.label: BC Regulation — Uses derating based on angle from the vertical (Table 15-3)
Industry best practice (ASME B30.9) — Do not use sling angles below 30° from the horizontal unless approved by the manufacturer or a qualified person

slides.focusCallout: Lower angle = higher leg tension.

slides.title: Calculator check — tension
slides.summary: Four quick problems. Use the tension multiplier chart. Work each before answers are revealed.

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the tension multiplier for a 45° sling angle (from horizontal)?
quizQuestions.explanation: At 45° from horizontal, the tension multiplier is 1.41 (L ÷ H or 1 ÷ sin 45°).

quizQuestions.prompt: A 6,000 lb load is on a 2-leg bridle. What is the load share per leg before sling angle is considered?
quizQuestions.explanation: Load per leg = Total Load ÷ Number of Loaded Legs → 6,000 ÷ 2 = 3,000 lb.

quizQuestions.prompt: 6,000 lb load · 2-leg bridle · 45° sling angle. What is the tension in each sling leg?
8,000 lb load · 2-leg bridle · 30° sling angle. What is the tension in each sling leg?
quizQuestions.explanation: Leg tension = (8,000 ÷ 2) × 2.00 = 4,000 × 2.00 = 8,000 lb — each leg carries the full load weight at 30°.

slides.title: Inspection & Removal Criteria
slides.summary: Remove a wire rope sling from service if any of the following are found:
slides.source: ASME B30.9 — Slings; WorkSafeBC OHSR Part 15

sections.heading: Remove from service

items.label: 10 or more randomly distributed broken wires in one rope lay, or
5 or more broken wires in one strand within one rope lay

items: Kinks, birdcaging, crushing, or core protrusion
Heat damage, arc strikes, or weld spatter

items.label: Severe corrosion or pitting

items: End attachments are cracked, deformed, loose, or damaged
Any reduction in rope diameter beyond the manufacturer's limits

items.label: Missing or illegible identification tag

sections.heading: Sling Identification Tag
items: Manufacturer's name or trademark
Manufacturer's code or stock number

items.label: Rated Working Load Limit (WLL) for applicable hitch(es)

items: Rope diameter
Number of legs (if multi-leg)
Reach (length) (multi-leg assemblies)
Legible identification tag

sections.heading: Remember
items: Inspect before each use

items.label: Never use a sling with a missing or illegible tag

items: Follow manufacturer removal criteria whenever they are more restrictive

slides.focusCallout: When in doubt, tag it out.

slides.title: Removal Criteria
slides.summary: Remove from service if any of the following are found:
slides.source: ASME B30.9 — Slings · WSTDA WS-1 — Synthetic Web Slings · WorkSafeBC OHSR Part 15

sections.heading: Remove from service

items.label: Missing or illegible identification tag

items: Acid or caustic burns
Melting, charring, or heat damage

items.label: Holes, tears, cuts, punctures, or snags

items: Broken or worn stitching in load-bearing splices
Excessive abrasive wear

items.label: Knots in any part of the sling

items: Discoloration, brittle fibers, or UV/environmental degradation affecting strength
Damaged fittings (bent, cracked, worn, or corroded)
Any manufacturer removal criteria are met

sections.heading: Required Identification Tag
items: Manufacturer's name or trademark
Manufacturer's code or stock number

items.label: Rated Working Load Limit (WLL) for applicable hitch(es)

items: Sling material (Nylon or Polyester)
Number of legs (if multi-leg)

sections.heading: Remember

items.label: No tag = No lift

items: Never tie knots to shorten a sling
Protect web slings from sharp edges with suitable edge protection

slides.focusCallout: No tag = No lift.

slides.title: Removal Criteria
slides.summary: Remove from service if any of the following are found:
slides.source: ASME B30.9 — Slings · WSTDA RS-1 — Synthetic Roundslings · WorkSafeBC OHSR Part 15

sections.heading: Remove from service

items.label: Missing or illegible identification tag
Cuts, tears, punctures, or snags in the cover
Exposed core yarns

items: Heat damage, melting, charring, or weld spatter
Acid or caustic burns
UV degradation, discoloration, or brittle fibers

items.label: Knots in the sling

items: Damaged fittings (if fitted)
Any manufacturer removal criteria are met

sections.heading: Required Identification Tag
items: Manufacturer's name or trademark
Manufacturer's code or stock number

items.label: Rated Working Load Limit (WLL) for applicable hitch(es)

items: Core material (where required by the manufacturer)
Number of legs (if multi-leg)

sections.heading: Remember

items.label: No tag = No lift
If the protective cover is damaged and the load-bearing core is exposed, remove the sling from service immediately

items: Protect round slings from sharp edges using appropriate edge protection

slides.focusCallout: No tag = No lift.

slides.title: Rated Hitch Capacities
slides.summary: *When the manufacturer's tag does not specify otherwise.
slides.source: ASME B30.9 — Section 9-2 (Synthetic Web Slings) and Section 9-6 (Polyester Roundslings)

sections.heading: Hitch ratings

items.label: Vertical · 100%

items: Choker – Web Sling · 75%
Choker – Polyester Roundsling · 80%

items.label: Basket · 200% (legs vertical & load balanced)

sections.heading: Important

items.label: Always use the WLL shown on the sling tag
If the tag does not list a choker rating: Synthetic Web Slings = 75% of the vertical rating; Polyester Roundslings = 80% of the vertical rating

items: For choke angles less than 120°, additional derating is required using the ASME B30.9 choke-angle tables or the manufacturer's instructions

slides.focusCallout: Always use the WLL shown on the sling tag.

slides.title: Common Hitch Configurations
slides.summary: *Typical rated capacity when the manufacturer's tag does not specify otherwise.
slides.source: ASME B30.9 — Slings · WSTDA WS-1 — Synthetic Web Slings · WSTDA RS-1 — Synthetic Roundslings

sections.heading: Configurations

items.label: Vertical Hitch · 100%
Basket Hitch · 200%

items: Basket Hitch (Inclined Legs) · Apply Sling Angle Derating
Single Choker Hitch · 75% (Web) / 80% (Roundsling)
Double-Leg Choker Hitch · 150% (Web) / 160% (Roundsling)

items.label: Double Wrap Hitch · Use Manufacturer's WLL

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Vertical Hitch – One sling leg supports the load
Basket Hitch – Up to 200% capacity when both legs are vertical and the load is balanced
Basket Hitch (Inclined Legs) – Capacity decreases as the sling angle decreases
Single Choker Hitch – Reduces capacity due to the choking action around the load

items.label: Double-Leg Choker Hitch – Twice the single choker rating (150% web / 160% roundsling)
Double Wrap Hitch – Increases grip; does not increase rated capacity unless the manufacturer specifically rates it

sections.heading: Remember

items.label: Always use the manufacturer's sling tag to determine the Working Load Limit (WLL)

items: Consider both the hitch type and sling angle when calculating sling capacity
Protect synthetic slings from sharp edges

slides.focusCallout: Always use the manufacturer's sling tag to determine WLL.

slides.title: Vertical vs Inclined Basket Legs
slides.summary: Vertical legs can use the full basket rating. Inclined legs require sling-angle derating.
slides.source: ASME B30.9 · WSTDA WS-1 / RS-1 · WorkSafeBC OHSR Part 15 · BC Crane Safety

sections.heading: 1. Vertical legs
items: Basket rating = up to 200% of the vertical hitch WLL
Both legs plumb and the load balanced

sections.heading: 2. Inclined legs
items: Do not use the full 200% basket rating
Effective capacity = Basket WLL ÷ (L ÷ H)  or  × sin(θ)

sections.heading: Best practices
items: Keep basket legs as vertical as practical
Use a spreader or longer reach when the angle would go shallow
Verify hitch type and angle against the sling tag WLL

slides.focusCallout: Inclined legs ≠ 200%. Apply sling-angle derating before you lift.

slides.title: Choke & basket math quiz
slides.summary: Use hitch ratings from the tag. Work each before revealing. Vertical WLL = 10,000 lb unless noted.

quizQuestions.prompt: Vertical WLL = 10,000 lb. Basket hitch with both legs vertical and the load balanced. What is the rated capacity?
quizQuestions.explanation: Vertical basket rating = 200% of vertical WLL → 10,000 × 2.00 = 20,000 lb.

quizQuestions.prompt: Vertical WLL = 10,000 lb. Single choker hitch on a synthetic web sling (choke angle ≥ 120°). What is the rated capacity?
quizQuestions.explanation: Web sling single choker ≈ 75% of vertical → 10,000 × 0.75 = 7,500 lb (unless the tag says otherwise).

quizQuestions.prompt: Vertical WLL = 10,000 lb. Single choker hitch on a polyester roundsling (choke angle ≥ 120°). What is the rated capacity?
quizQuestions.explanation: Polyester roundsling single choker ≈ 80% of vertical → 10,000 × 0.80 = 8,000 lb.

quizQuestions.prompt: One web sling in a double-leg (two-leg) choker hitch. Vertical WLL = 10,000 lb. What is the rated capacity?
quizQuestions.explanation: One-sling / double-leg choker = 2 × single choker. Web: 2 × 75% = 150% → 10,000 × 1.50 = 15,000 lb. (Roundsling: 2 × 80% = 160%.)

slides.title: Double Wrap & Double Choke
slides.summary: Loads made of 2 or more pieces over 3 m (10 ft) long must be double wrapped and choked — OHSR 15.40.

items.label: 2 or more pieces of material
Each piece over 3 m (10 ft) long

sections.heading: Required method

items.label: Use a 2-legged sling arrangement

items: Position the slings to keep the load horizontal during the lift

items.label: Each sling must be choked around the load with a double wrap

sections.heading: Remember

items.label: Double wrap + choke = grip that resists slip and bundle shift

items: Select and use the sling to prevent slipping or overstressing (OHSR 15.40(1))
Protect synthetic slings from sharp edges

slides.focusCallout: 2+ pieces · over 3 m (10 ft) · double wrap · choke each sling

slides.title: Never Hammer a Choke
slides.summary: Never force the choke eye down the sling. Hammering creates an acute choke angle, damages the sling, and drops capacity below the tagged choker rating.
slides.source: ASME B30.9 · WSTDA WS-1 / RS-1 / RS-2 · manufacturer instructions · WorkSafeBC OHSR Part 15

items.label: 120°–180° · × 1.00 (full choker rating)

sections.heading: Why never hammer

items.label: Forces an acute choke angle well below 120°

items: Damages sling body fibres at the choke point
Creates an unrated condition — capacity may be lower than any table predicts
Let the choke settle naturally as the load is lifted

sections.heading: Standards & practice

items.label: ASME B30.9 — choke angles under 120° need manufacturer or qualified-person ratings

items: WSTDA WS-1 / RS-1 / RS-2 — multiply choker WLL by the angle reduction factor
Manufacturer instructions — do not hammer, pound, or force a choke
OHSR Part 15 — select and use slings to prevent slipping or overstressing

slides.focusCallout: Let the choke set naturally — never hammer it down.
